chapters.title: The Somalis were well prepared. The Americans were hanging on for dear life
chapters.text:
As a child, Tom Satterly was bullied by older children who mercilessly ridiculed him for his skinny frame and his love for playing the violin. Nobody would have guessed that Satterly would grow up to be a top-ranking Command Sgt. Major in the US military, not least take part in some of the world’s most notorious and bloody battles to free innocent civilians.

Growing up in a small town in Indiana, Satterly’s brother encouraged him to fight back against the bullies and stand up for himself, but he didn’t believe he could do it. However, one day, he snapped and couldn’t take any more of their incessant name-calling and pushing. Having fought the bullies off and saved his friend, Satterly felt he could take on the world and win.

Having defeated the enemy (the bullies), Tom Satterly told himself he would never run away from danger again.

As he reached his early adult years, Satterly had his mindset on working in construction, but he had become lazy and didn’t know how to get started. He began watching old war movies and thinking war seemed to be heroic because of the welcome home parades he saw in the films. He never really thought at the time about the cost of human life. That would come much later.

In February 1986, Satterly’s love of war movies took a turn and changed his life forever. He enlisted into the army and reported to basic training at Fort Leonard Wood in Missouri. It was hard work, but he relished the challenge and enjoyed learning how to shoot a gun.

Working as part of a team with other young soldiers, Satterly felt like he finally belonged. He was now part of something much bigger than himself and realized he had found his purpose — protecting his country and standing up for those who couldn’t stand up for themselves, just as he had done in the face of persistent bullying.

In this summary, you will learn more about Tom Satterly’s fascinating and heartbreaking story of bravery in the face of death and the mental scars he struggles with to this day.

chapters.title: The push to be the best will always take its toll
chapters.text:
Although military service seemed uplifting, the command did not try to create a false sense of nonchalance amongst recruits. Boot camp showed Satterly snapshots of what he could expect during his career in the army. However, it certainly didn’t prepare him for the toils and terrors of war in the real world. During his younger army days, everything seemed exciting, but he never stopped to imagine the reality of the situations he would be facing shortly.

His career seemed to speed up after hearing about a mysterious part of the army called the Unit. The Unit is a special missions section of the military, responsible for some of the world’s most dangerous assignments, including killing terrorists, freeing hostages, and in many cases, taking down violent drug cartels. But unfortunately, they only recruited the very best, and he realized that it would take years before he could reach that point. So in the meantime, Satterly decided he wanted to try out for the Special Forces and become a Green Beret.

The motto of the Green Berets is ‘de oppresso liber,’ which translates as ‘liberate the oppressed.’

In August 1989, Satterly was accepted to the selection process and began his intensive 28-day training course, following which he successfully graduated.

However, his life was to change once again as he was approached by two mysterious army personnel, mentioning the Unit in whispered tones. They felt he would be a good pick and encouraged him to apply for the selection process. Satterly wasn’t entirely sure what he had signed up for, but he knew it would be extreme and, if successful, would mean he was considered one of the very best in the US military. He finally felt as if he belonged.

I liked the whole mystique and secrecy surrounding the Unit and was elated that I had been noticed and invited to try out. It was supposed to be the best of the best and that appealed to me. ~ Tom Satterly

The selection process for the Unit pushed his mind and body to their limits. At this point, Satterly realized that you have to go beyond anything you thought you were capable of to be the best. He didn’t know it at the time, but all of this would have severe consequences for his future physical and mental health.

chapters.title: Much danger lurked in Mogadishu: a story of a terrifying and perilous task
chapters.text:
Having been sent on minor tasks since successfully being enlisted into the Unit, Tom Satterly was about to have his very first taste of real battle and war. At the time, a bloody civil war was raging in Somalia at the hands of warlord Mohamed Farrah Aidid.

The country plunged into severe drought and famine after overthrowing the government, and when UN peacekeepers were sent to distribute aid to the needy, they were ambushed by violent militia. The capital city, Mogadishu, was where much of the trouble was located, sitting on the Horn of Africa against the harsh desert and the glittering Arabian Sea.

Safety is a necessity and right of every person, but sometimes it comes at a difficult and expensive price.

It was increasingly likely that Satterly’s C squadron would be called upon to travel to Mogadishu and take part in a special operation to capture the warlord. The training was intense and involved Black Hawk helicopter practice; the Black Hawk is a military aircraft that provides support and military assistance during conflicts. The military knew that the battle would be brutal and bloody if they were called into action in Somalia. The militia would use civilians as human shields, and it would involve a lot of urban warfare, the most difficult of all.

The call finally came in July 1993, when President Bill Clinton ordered Task Force Ranger into Mogadishu to capture or kill Aidid and his top-ranking lieutenants. US raids so far had been unsuccessful and resulted in severe loss of life.

Upon arriving in Mogadishu, Satterly immediately knew that extreme danger lurked around every corner. He mused that even the sea was dangerous and packed with sharks. Although excited to get started on the mission, Satterly had a sinking feeling that this would be a dangerous and highly troublesome task – he was yet to understand just how terrifying it would be.

Did you know? The battle in Mogadishu was the basis for the Hollywood blockbuster Black Hawk Down. The movie grossed a massive $172 million worldwide.

chapters.title: An operation gone wrong caused death and destruction
chapters.text:
Several small operations in Mogadishu had yielded results, including capturing one of Aidid’s top lieutenants; however, this only intensified the warlord’s efforts. In September 1993, the militia had successfully shot down a Black Hawk belonging to the 10th Mountain Division. The event was a significant win for Aidid, as this was the first time a US military helicopter had been shot down, inflicting heavy US casualties.

The squadron was focusing their preparations on what to do in the event of a downed helicopter, as it seemed this was a particular focal point for Aidid’s men.

No matter how much we prepare for difficulties, reality is more complicated. The best thing we can do is learn from it and not give up.

One particular afternoon, Satterly and his squadron received word of a meeting between top-ranking officials and potentially Aidid himself at the Olympic Hotel in central Mogadishu. The squadron was mobilized and set into action. However, from the start, heavy dust that kicked up from the helicopters caused problems with visibility and landing. Aidid’s militia sprang into action when they noticed the incoming attack; they moved far faster than the US expected. From the start, they found themselves under extremely heavy fire, with the support of hostile Somali citizens on the streets.

In the first few minutes of the operation, Satterly killed more people than he had in his entire career. As urban fighting raged on, the US suffered heavy personnel losses, and then the “impossible” happened — another Black Hawk was shot down. They knew they had to get to the helicopter as quickly as possible to save the remaining crew and stop the militia from taking anything useful from the wreckage.

While the squadron had received downed helicopter training just a few days before, nothing could prepare them for the extreme circumstances and violence firing at them from all directions. The militia was surrounding the crash site, and as night began to fall, the squadron found themselves in deep trouble, having left their night vision goggles back at the base. What was supposed to be a quick operation had turned into a fight for life and death.

It was going to be a long night, and we’d be lucky to survive it. I’d had my fill of combat — of blood, and fear, and death — and I wanted no more of it. ~ Tom Satterly

chapters.title: The Somalis were well prepared. The Americans were hanging on for dear life
chapters.text:
Not only was the US fighting the violent militia, but a mobilized population too; they had drastically underestimated how prepared the Somalis were. Their situation was not helped by the fact that, in the eyes of the Somali locals, the US soldiers were now occupiers in their country, so they were extremely hostile and sided with Aidid’s men.

A few years later, the truth came to light about how the Somalis were so prepared. Al-Qaeda, a growing Islamic extremist group headed by Osama Bin Laden, had taught the militia how to fight and use rocket-propelled grenades (RPGs) to devastating effect. These RPGs had successfully knocked two Black Hawks out of the sky, a significant coup for the militia group.

Despite the mounting deaths on the Somali side, Aidid refused to curb fighting and only called for its escalation.

Word had also quickly spread to the militia and local community about the US plan to strike the Olympic Hotel. Within minutes, trucks of fighters were on the scene, and the ambush was intense. Everything seemed to be going against the US at that point, clinging on for dear life and desperately trying to reach the crash site to save the remaining crew — if they were still alive. Nevertheless, every American failure was a significant win for the Somalis, increasing their morale and forcing them to continue their bombardment.

Satterly was extremely worried about the fate of his friends, but he wouldn’t allow himself to dwell on it, intensely focused on getting to the site and getting out of there alive. As fighting continued into the early morning hours, the smell of burning flesh and dead bodies flashed through his mind. The night seemed to go on forever, and the thought that he was glad he had written to his parents the day before flicked through his mind.

Communication with loved ones can be a lifeline, especially in crucial situations.

At that point, surviving this bloody and violent battle seemed incomprehensible. There seemed to be no way out.

chapters.title: There is no glory in war, only the stench of rotting flesh
chapters.text:
The endless night finally broke into light as dawn approached. The city was burned out, and the sound of gunfire pierced the scene. Smoke billowed as cars burned, the acrid smell of metal and flesh assaulting the senses. But then, something happened.

Satterly could make out the movement of tanks in the distance. He thought this odd as tanks were not supposed to be used in urban streets due to their size. Yet, he could see them moving toward him. Then he realized the cavalry was on its way.

The 10th Mountain Division had sought the help of Malaysian and Pakistani forces, who had agreed to join the battle to reach the Black Hawk crash site. In movies, what came next was referred to as the ‘Mogadishu Mile,’ but in reality, the distance to the crash site was far more than a mile.

Even when the odds seem stacked against you, it’s important to keep moving forward.

As they made their way toward the site, Satterly remembered seeing an Apache attack helicopter overhead. He stopped for a split second to look up and then found himself surrounded. Everyone had moved ahead, and he was alone, encircled by Somali citizens — some hostile, some terrified. He had made a mistake, simply looking up for one second.

Just as he was making his peace with his end, a hand grabbed him from behind and yanked him into an alleyway. Seconds later, an RPG hit the spot where he had been standing. A medic named Kurt had saved his life, and the near-death experience was enough to shake him out of his momentary fog and push him to catch up with the rest of the squadron.

When they reached the crash site, the vision shocked him to his very core. He had seen death before, but this was on a different scale. The bodies of US soldiers lay all around him, and he could not dare look too closely in case he recognized one of their faces. Sand had been poured over the bodies to soak up the blood, and the smell of bleach was hanging heavily in the air — an attempt to sanitize the area.

The sight haunts him even to this day.

chapters.title: A hero on the battlefield, a hero at home
chapters.text:
The Battle of Mogadishu (also known as the Black Hawk incident) was not Tom Satterly’s only extreme assignment, but it was the one that pierced his mind. It remained with him, mainly due to the scents he remembers from his time in Somalia.

To him, Somalia was about acrid, rotting scents from decapitated corpses and littered body parts. It was the screams and the sight of blood soaking into the sand. He would never forget it.

On October 13, 1993, Clinton ordered the cessation of operations in Somalia. On the flight back over the Atlantic, Satterly felt changed. His experiences in Mogadishu had altered his mindset and life forever. It wasn’t known at the time, but the success of Aidid’s men over the US also gave significant hope to Al Qaeda. History shows what happened next.

Tom Satterly was posted to other trouble spots worldwide, including Bosnia and the infamous mission to Iraq, tasked with capturing or killing Saddam Hussein.

Something had changed. The initial desire Satterly had within him to protect and fight was waning. He was physically and mentally exhausted, living on a cocktail of antidepressants and pain medications.

The haunting memories and ghosts of the past returned to him as he slept, pushing him deep into depression and causing him to have suicidal thoughts. He saw the faces of the dead in his dreams and heard the screams of those suffering.

It all pushed him to the brink after many years of extreme stress and fear, living in a constant state of adrenaline, and never knowing if he would see his loved ones again. He remembers thinking about Valhalla on the way back from the special mission to Iraq. Valhalla is a version of heaven in Norse myths, specifically for warriors. It was a way for soldiers to know that they would see their brothers again in Valhalla if they didn’t make it. Yet, Satterly didn’t want to go to Valhalla anymore. He wanted peace.

Tom Satterly was later diagnosed with PTSD and heroically lives with several debilitating injuries from his days on the frontline. A true hero and savior for so many, he struggles with the memories of his past.

War isn’t glorious. It serves no one, not least those who live with the ghosts every day.

chapters.title: Conclusion
chapters.text:
History is littered with famous battles, many of which have been immortalized into Hollywood folklore. If you have ever watched the movie Black Hawk Down, you will have a tiny idea of what happened during those violent and bloody days. But, you will only have the slightest insight. In reality, the battle was more extreme than we know, only understood by those in the thick of it.

We rightly celebrate our veterans. We salute them for their bravery and willingness to lay down their lives to protect us. But, we give little thought to how this affects them at the time and later in their lives.

Tom Satterly lives with PTSD. He struggles with the haunting memories of every person he had to kill, the faces of his fallen brothers, and the terrible flashbacks of battlefields soaked with blood and corpses. It is not something you can forget, yet he bravely puts one foot forward every day and continues to live.

That is true heroism.

War isn’t like what you see on TV; it’s not the same as the Hollywood movies; war is a hell that cannot be described, a fight against evil. In a world that seems to celebrate war, we need to pull our minds back to its reality. Death, destruction, displacement — nothing will ever be the same again.

So, let’s raise a glass to our veterans, the heroes who bravely stand in the face of danger to keep us safe and protected, even if it costs them their health and happiness for the rest of their days.

Try this

• Understanding world events is essential, but focusing too much on negative news can affect your mental health. Limit your exposure if you feel overwhelmed.
• Once their career in the military is over, veterans require our support. Do you know a veteran you can help today?
• PTSD is a severe condition. Learn the signs so you can spot them in your loved ones.
